sourceMovie.title: The Conjuring: Last Rites
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: People revisit The Conjuring: Last Rites for the same reason it broke through in 2025: clear stakes, memorable faces, and a horror rhythm that doesn't stall in the middle. Michael Chaves keeps the ensemble pointed in one direction. The film leans hard into its core genre, which is exactly what fans say they wanted more of from blockbusters in that era.

recommendations.title: The Conjuring
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Fans of The Conjuring: Last Rites who respond to its horror will find The Conjuring (2013) immediately familiar. Both films share a thriller sensibility without ever feeling like carbon copies of each other.

recommendations.title: The Conjuring: The Devil Made Me Do It
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: There's a reason The Conjuring: Last Rites fans keep landing on The Conjuring: The Devil Made Me Do It (2021) — the horror is unmistakably related even when the plots diverge. Both films operate on the same emotional frequency, especially in their third acts.

recommendations.title: Halloween
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Halloween draws from the same horror well as The Conjuring: Last Rites, channeling thriller in ways that feel unmistakably related. The 2018 film makes a natural companion watch — similar in spirit but distinctly its own.

recommendations.title: Scream 2
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Scream 2 (1997) captures the horror appeal of The Conjuring: Last Rites without simply imitating it. The mystery streak running through both films is what puts Scream 2 at the top of any "if you liked…" shortlist.

recommendations.title: Insidious: Chapter 2
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The horror appeal of The Conjuring: Last Rites — that dark edge, the thriller — translates cleanly into Insidious: Chapter 2 (2013). Different setting, same essential satisfaction.

recommendations.title: Insidious: Chapter 3
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The filmmakers behind Insidious: Chapter 3 (2015) clearly understood what makes horror work — the same quality that keeps The Conjuring: Last Rites rewatchable. Expect the same dark rhythm, executed with a fresh angle.

recommendations.title: Weapons
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Weapons (2025) is the kind of film The Conjuring: Last Rites fans discover and immediately send to their group chat. The horror overlap is real, but it's the dark emotional pull that seals the recommendation.

recommendations.title: Insidious: The Last Key
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Insidious: The Last Key shares The Conjuring: Last Rites's gift for horror — the 2018 film delivers thriller without overstaying its welcome. Consider it required viewing once you've finished rewatching your first pick.

recommendations.title: The Pope's Exorcist
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Much of what makes The Conjuring: Last Rites compelling — the horror, the thriller — shows up in The Pope's Exorcist (2023) in a slightly different form. Both films understand that dark storytelling works best when it trusts the audience.

recommendations.title: 30 Days of Night
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The horror energy of The Conjuring: Last Rites is alive in 30 Days of Night (2007), filtered through a different story but the same emotional register. It earns its place on this list with a dark payoff that rivals the original pick.

faq.question: What should I watch after The Conjuring: Last Rites?
faq.answer: Try The Conjuring, The Conjuring: The Devil Made Me Do It, Halloween—each shares DNA with The Conjuring: Last Rites while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is The Conjuring: Last Rites?
faq.answer: The Conjuring: Last Rites (2025) is best described as Horror. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is The Conjuring: Last Rites part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "The Conjuring Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
